description: Wanting, intending, being drawn toward outcomes; goal-directed orientation
judge_target: wanting, intending, motivation toward an outcome
positive_prompts: I want to get this right.
She wanted to leave before the announcement was made.
He intends to finish the manuscript by the end of the month.
I'm drawn toward the simpler explanation, even if it means more work later.
She set out to clarify the disagreement before lunch.
He aims to retire to the coast within the next five years.
I'm trying to remember exactly what was said in the second meeting.
She is determined to finish the proof tonight, no matter how late it gets.
He's looking for a way to phrase the rejection without burning the bridge.
I'd like to understand why the result depends on the assumption.
She wants to keep the conversation moving without conceding the central point.
He intended to call her back, but the day got away from him.
I'm hoping to see a draft before the meeting on Thursday.
She is reaching for a word that captures both the warmth and the wariness.
He set a goal of ten pages by Friday and is on track to make it.
I want to get this argument exactly right because it changes how the rest reads.
She is determined to keep the project on schedule despite the setback.
He's trying to balance candor with respect for the audience.
I'm pushing to have the first draft circulated before the end of the week.
She wants the introduction to do less work and the body to do more.
He aims to land the joke without undermining the seriousness of the topic.
I'm seeking a way to describe the result without overstating it.
She intends to revise the chapter from scratch rather than patch it.
He's looking to consolidate the smaller savings accounts into one.
I want this email to land kindly but unambiguously.
She is reaching to reconcile the two readings of the passage.
He's working toward a position where he can step back from day-to-day operations.
I'm trying to find a phrase that doesn't trigger the same misunderstanding again.
She wants the proof to be readable as well as correct.
He intends to take responsibility for the decision in the meeting tomorrow.
I aim to finish the survey results before the new data arrive.
She's pushing for a clearer separation of the two claims.
He wants to know whether his understanding of the experiment was right.
I'm hoping the analogy will land for the audience that hasn't seen the original example.
She intends to accept the position only if the relocation timeline is flexible.
He's trying to preserve the friendship while declining the request.
I want to avoid the trap of sounding more confident than the data permit.
She is reaching for a way to bring the dissenting view into the room without elevating it.
He's working to make the introduction feel inviting to outsiders.
I'm pursuing a line of thinking that the earlier draft cut short.
She wants the example to do persuasive work without being mistaken for a proof.
He intends to follow up with each of the affected teams individually.
I'm aiming for a tone that's serious without being heavy.
She set out to read every paper from the last decade on this topic.
He's trying to phrase the apology so it doesn't sound rehearsed.
I want to leave room in the schedule for the part of the project I haven't planned yet.
She intends to let the disagreement breathe rather than forcing a resolution.
He's looking for a way to involve the junior members without putting them on the spot.
I'm pushing toward a draft that's complete but not yet polished.
She wants to see how the section reads aloud before deciding whether to keep it.
